title: Cyclic Voltammetry in the Spleen for In Vivo Recording of Stimulus-Evoked Norepinephrine
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"2m1sf","text":"This protocol describes methodology for in vivo recording of norepinephrine concentration in the spleen of mice during a stimulus (i.e electrical nerve stimulation, drug injection, etc.). Part 1 describes the preparation of surgical and recording equipment. Part 2 describes the surgery and recording. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dblf2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"32ql5","text":"The typical voltammetric signal for norepinephrine in the spleen is characterized in Figure 1 of the referenced manuscript. Stimulation of the splenic, splanchnic, or vagus nerves with a 10-second electrical train should illicit a rising signal that plateus in ~1min and comes back to baseline in ~1-2 minutes. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"1sd5v","text":"For Part 1:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3bsqm","text":"Purchase Pinnacle fast-scan cyclic voltammetry system and consult set-up instructions. Purchase disposable Pinnacle carbon fiber micro-electrodes, silver wire, and bleach solution. Gather wooden cotton-tip rods or popsicle sticks, ParaFilm, and adhesive. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2i6cp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"137c1","text":"For Part 2:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3vdvq","text":"Check isoflurane and oxygen tank levels in anesthesia vaporizer, decontaminate surgical field and instruments, allow heating pad to come to temperature.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"7dt61","text":"Carbon Fiber Electrode is highly fragile, especially to sheer forces. Always face electrode tips away from movement as loose clothing brushing against it can destroy it. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bff2v","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c37j3","text":"FSCV recordings are sensitive to movement. Breathing motions can create artifacts, spleen must be stabilized as much as possible before inserting electrodes.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"21vqf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"atctn","text":"Once punctured, FSCV electrodes cannot be removed from the spleen without major bleeding, so remove electrodes only once animal can be sacrificed. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Assemble electrode support rig using any suitable materials and adhesives, such aswooden applicators sticks and tape (Figure 1 – Component B)

Cut off wire lead from any used or broken Pinnacle electrode or reference to obtain headboard pin connector. Cut 10cm of silver wire and solder it to exposed wire from headboard pin connector to obtain custom Pinnacle reference electrode. Sharpen silver wire tip using a scalpel, than submerge in fresh bleach solution for 1 hour to obtain custom Ag/Cl reference. (Figure 1 – Component A) Figure 1 : Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry implant components. Component A : Ag/AgCl electrode, sharpened to pierce splenic capsule. Component B : Wooden rig to stabilize electrodes. Component C : Carbon-fiber microelectrode from Pinnacle.

Attach custom sharpened Ag/Cl reference electrode to tip of support rig using ParaFilm, ensure at least 5cm of clearance between tip of support rig and sharpened tip of reference electrode. Wrap ParaFilm at two attachment points, one near the tip of the support rig, such that reference electrode is rigidly bound to the support rig.

Gently remove Pinnacle carbon-fiber electrode from packaging and attach to support rig using ParaFilm, same as reference electrode. (Figure 1 – Component C) Tip of sharp Ag reference electrode should be protruding just ahead of the tip of carbon fiber and 1 cm apart from each other. Both electrodes should be rigidly attached to support rig. (Figure 1)

Mount Pinnacle headboard to stereotax so that it can be moved precisely during implantation procedure. (Figure 2A). Then attach electrode support rig to sterotax and connect reference and carbon fiber electrodes to headboard (Figure 2B) Figure 2 : Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry rig for recording and implantation. Stereotax for implant manipulation. Pinnacle voltage driver and headboard for recording. Support rig to stabilize electrodes during implantation.
Connect headboard to Pinnacle voltage driver device (see Pinnacle instructions for set up).
Induce anesthesia with 4% isoflurane. Lay animal dorsum on heating pad and maintain anesthesia at 1.5%.
The following instruction split depending on the type of stimulation that will be used to evoke NE release in the spleen.
Shave cervical region and make midline incision.
Lift left parotid gland to expose left anterior triangle of the neck. Left sternocleidomastoid and esophagus should be visible.
Use microhook to lift vagus nerve up and over a Cortec stimulating electrode cuff. Seal vagus nerve on cuff with Quik-Sil elastomer to prevent movement or current leakage.

Place three cotton tips under the spleen, taped down to the heating pad as shown in Figure 3A. Elevate the middle cottontip by inserting a wooden stick between the cottontips until the splenic neurovascular bundle is taught. This rig should dampen the movement of the spleen with the animal’s breathing and hold it rigidly in place. Ensure that spleen surface is dry. Figure 3: A) Stabilization of spleen using micropoint cottontips. B) Exposed spleen with FSCV electrodes inserted and Cortec stimulation electrode on splenic nerve.

Position the electrode tips above the spleen. Use stereotax to slowly descend electrodes until they touch the splenic capsule. Then alternate moving electrodes downward and midline until the reference and carbon fiber pierce the splenic capsule (Figure 3B). Once pierced, depress electrodes slightly more and do not move any further. If pierced spleen begins bleeding uncontrollably, procedure will have to be restarted. Otherwise, cover spleen and exposed abdominal organs in damp KimWipe, carefully avoiding any motion of submerged electrodes.
Open Pinnacle FSCV application and turn on continuous cycling. Allow electrodes to record for 10 minutes, undisturbed, for conditioning.

Record voltammetry signal continuously while delivering electrical stimulation trains to electrode leads (30Hz, bipolar, 10-200μA, 5-100 seconds). Allow voltammetry signal to return to baseline before delivering next stimulus.
Retract sternocleidomastoid to expose the carotid sheath. Gently blunt dissect the carotid sheath between internal carotid and left vagus nerve to separate left vagus nerve.

Make incision under left costal margin through skin and external and internal oblique muscles to reach peritoneal cavity. (shown in Figure 3A) Gently massage spleen out of the peritoneal cavity using wet cotton tips.

Place three cotton tips under the spleen, taped down to the heating pad as shown in Figure 3A. Elevate the middle cottontip by inserting a wooden stick between the cottontips until the splenic neurovascular bundle is taught. This rig should dampen the movement of the spleen with the animal’s breathing and hold it rigidly in place. Ensure that spleen surface is dry. Figure 3: A) Stabilization of spleen using micropoint cottontips. B) Exposed spleen with FSCV electrodes inserted and Cortec stimulation electrode on splenic nerve.

Position the electrode tips above the spleen. Use stereotax to slowly descend electrodes until they touch the splenic capsule. Then alternate moving electrodes downward and midline until the reference and carbon fiber pierce the splenic capsule (Figure 3B). Once pierced, depress electrodes slightly more and do not move any further. If pierced spleen begins bleeding uncontrollably, procedure will have to be restarted. Otherwise, cover spleen and exposed abdominal organs in damp KimWipe, carefully avoiding any motion of submerged electrodes.
Open Pinnacle FSCV application and turn on continuous cycling. Allow electrodes to record for 10 minutes, undisturbed, for conditioning.

Record voltammetry signal continuously while delivering electrical stimulation trains to electrode leads (30Hz, bipolar, 100-500μA, 5-100 seconds). Allow voltammetry signal to return to baseline before delivering next stimulus.

Make incision under left costal margin through skin and external and internal oblique muscles to reach peritoneal cavity. (shown in Figure 3A) Gently massage spleen out of the peritoneal cavity using wet cotton tips.

Find splenic neurovascular bundle entering the posterior pole of the spleen. Blunt dissect peritoneal fat away from neurovascular bundle and use microhook to lift neurovascular bundle up and over a Cortec stimulating electrode (pictured in Figure 3B). Then seal splenic neurovascular bundle in Quik-Sil elastomer to prevent current leakage and movement.

Position the electrode tips above the spleen. Use stereotax to slowly descend electrodes until they touch the splenic capsule. Then alternate moving electrodes downward and midline until the reference and carbon fiber pierce the splenic capsule (Figure 4C). Once pierced, depress electrodes slightly more and do not move any further. If pierced spleen begins bleeding uncontrollably, procedure will have to be restarted. Otherwise, cover spleen and exposed abdominal organs in damp KimWipe, carefully avoiding any motion of submerged electrodes.
Open Pinnacle FSCV application and turn on continuous cycling. Allow electrodes to record for 10 minutes, undisturbed, for conditioning.

Record voltammetry signal continuously while delivering electrical stimulation trains to electrode leads (30Hz, bipolar, 100-500μA, 5-100 seconds). Allow voltammetry signal to return to baseline before delivering next stimulus.

Recommended to use ChAT-TdTomato mouse breed to visualize celiac ganglion and splanchnic nerves using fluorescence microscope. Breed ChAT–IRES-Cre (#006410) and Ai14 ROSA–tdTomato (#007914) from Jackson Laboratory to produce ChAT-TdTomato pups. Make a midline incision into the abdomen along the linea alba. Externalize the peritoneal fat and intestines and use retractors to pull them to the anatomical right of the animal. This should expose the posterior abdominal cavity, with the abdominal aorta and left kidney visible. (Figure 3A) Figure 3: A) Posterior abdomen exposed with stimulating electrode place under splanchnic nerve. B) Zoomed view shown in (A) showing slightly translucent celiac ganglion and splanchnic nerve over celiac artery branching from aorta. C) Same view as (B) showing ChAT-Tdtomato fluorescence microscope view.

Zoom and focus onto the celiac artery branching from the abdominal aorta. Then, dim lights and switch from brightfield to fluorescence view in fluorescence dissecting microscope. Celiac ganglion and splanchnic nerves should now be visible under green fluorescent light with red filter. Alternatively, celiac ganglion may be located in brightfield view, but this is difficult and unreliable.

The splanchnic nerve enters to the celiac ganglion superiorly and is often partially covered by the left kidney. (Figure 3B) It is faintly visible under fluorescent light and often requires zoomed view for good visualization. Push the kidney to the anatomical left using retractors and blunt dissect splanchnic nerve until it is separate from surrounding fascia. Then, lift nerve up using microhook and pull over a Cortec stimulating electrode cuff. Seal the nerve in Quik-Sil elastomer to prevent movement and current leakage, as fluid often builds in this cavity and can disrupt electrical stimulation. WARNING: Splanchnic nerve often runs over the celiac artery and aorta. Fluorescent view maximizes visualization of the nerve but minimizes visualization of blood vessels and vice versa for brightfield. It is recommended to toggle between brightfield and fluorescent view to get familiar with positioning of vessels relative to nerve before blunt dissecting in fluorescent view to avoid rupturing a vessel.

After allowing Quik-Sil elastomer to cure, pull spleen and intestines back over to the anatomical left, covering the splanchnic nerve and celiac ganglion. Prepare spleen stablization rig using MicroPoint cotton tips (Figure 4A) and drape spleen and attached peritoneal fat onto elevated cotton tip so that the spleen is secure and not moving with breathing of the animal (Figure 4B) Figure 4 : A) Spleen stabilization rig using MicroPoint cottontips. B) Spleen draped over spleen stabilization rig dissected away from peritoneal fat and overlooking cuffed splanchnic nerve. C) FSCV electrodes pierced through splenic capsule

Place three cotton tips under the spleen, taped down to the heating pad as shown in Figure 3A. Elevate the middle cottontip by inserting a wooden stick between the cottontips until the splenic neurovascular bundle is taught. This rig should dampen the movement of the spleen with the animal’s breathing and hold it rigidly in place. Ensure that spleen surface is dry. Figure 3: A) Stabilization of spleen using micropoint cottontips. B) Exposed spleen with FSCV electrodes inserted and Cortec stimulation electrode on splenic nerve.

Position the electrode tips above the spleen. Use stereotax to slowly descend electrodes until they touch the splenic capsule. Then alternate moving electrodes downward and midline until the reference and carbon fiber pierce the splenic capsule (Figure 3B). Once pierced, depress electrodes slightly more and do not move any further. If pierced spleen begins bleeding uncontrollably, procedure will have to be restarted. Otherwise, cover spleen and exposed abdominal organs in damp KimWipe, carefully avoiding any motion of submerged electrodes.
Open Pinnacle FSCV application and turn on continuous cycling. Allow electrodes to record for 10 minutes, undisturbed, for conditioning.
Record voltammetry signal continuously while infusing increasing concentrations of norepinephrine in 100μL boluses at a time. Allow voltammetry signal to return to baseline between boluses.
Shave cervical region and make midline incision.
Lift left parotid gland to expose left anterior triangle of the neck. Left sternocleidomastoid and esophagus should be visible.
Retract skin to the anatomical left of incision to visualize external jugular vein partially occluded by fat. Gently blunt dissect surrounding fat until vein is isolated.

Make incision under left costal margin through skin and external and internal oblique muscles to reach peritoneal cavity. (shown in Figure 3A) Gently massage spleen out of the peritoneal cavity using wet cotton tips.
Using silk suture, tie external jugular vein superiorly and pull until taught. Secure silk suture so that vein remains taught.
Loop silk suture around exposed vein inferiorly such that it is loose and can be tied to secure the catheter once it is inserted.

Puncture vein with a 30 gauge needle bent at a 90 degree angle, and while needle is inserted, pull needle midline to loosen hole in the vein. Then, with free hand, insert 1French catheter into hole opened by the needle. Remove needle and tie loose suture around catheter to secure it in the vein. WARNING : Catheter must be filled with fluid. There can be no air in the catheter. Pushing air into the vein can cause air embolism and kill animal.
Push 50 μL of saline through catheter while watching vein. If reflux occurs, reinsert catheter and try again.
creator: Michael Gerber
